name: Hoodoo
description: Hoodoo, also known as conjure, rootwork, or tricking, is an African American folk magic tradition that arose from the blending of various African spiritual practices brought to North America by enslaved Africans. It incorporates elements of traditional African religions, European folk magic, Native American herbalism, and Christian beliefs. Hoodoo is primarily a system of practical magic, focusing on achieving tangible results in everyday life through spells, charms, and rituals. It emphasizes working with spirits, roots, herbs, and other natural objects to influence events and people. Unlike some other African diaspora religions, Hoodoo does not have a priesthood or central authority, and practitioners typically learn through family tradition, apprenticeship, or self-study. It is deeply rooted in the history and culture of African Americans and continues to be practiced today, often in secret or alongside other religious beliefs. Hoodoo focuses on personal empowerment and problem-solving, offering remedies for a wide range of issues, including love, money, health, and protection. The tradition is often misunderstood and conflated with other practices, but it maintains its distinct identity as a powerful and resilient form of African American folk magic.
subtitle: African American folk magic tradition.

keyMyths.title: The Origin of Rootwork
keyMyths.description: Rootwork came to America with enslaved Africans, adapting and evolving with new surroundings. The knowledge of roots and herbs was used for healing and resistance.

sources.author: Zora Neale Hurston
sources.description: An anthropological study of African American folklore, including Hoodoo practices.
sources.text: An anthropological study of African American folklore, including Hoodoo practices.

sources.author: Harry Middleton Hyatt
